sources.notes: Statement and constraints: reconstruct an orientation of a tree from a reachability matrix; easy version has small enough n for cubic checks.
Used to confirm the transitive-reduction criterion: an edge u->v exists iff u reaches v and no third vertex lies between them in reachability order.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate Luogu solution page; not used as evidence in this review.

annotation.summary: Given the reachability matrix of an unknown directed orientation of an undirected tree, reconstruct the directed tree or report impossibility.
annotation.constraints: Easy version permits direct O(n^3) reasoning over triples of vertices.
annotation.core_idea: The reachability relation of an oriented tree is a partial order. A directed edge u->v is exactly a cover relation: u reaches v, u != v, and there is no third vertex w such that u reaches w and w reaches v. Therefore scan all ordered pairs and add the pair as an edge when no intermediate vertex exists. After constructing these cover edges, verify that the undirected graph is a tree and that its reachability matches the input matrix.
annotation.complexity: O(n^3) to test all possible intermediates and verify reachability.
tricks: view the reachability matrix as a partial order

solution_variants.name: cubic-transitive-reduction
solution_variants.summary: For every reachable ordered pair, check all possible intermediate vertices. Pairs without an intermediate are exactly the directed edges.

tags.evidence: The reconstructed edges are exactly the cover relations of the reachability partial order, i.e. the transitive reduction of the directed tree.
tags.description: Problems that recover or use the minimal edge set preserving reachability in a DAG-like relation.
tags.created_reason: This is more precise than generic graph/tree tags and is reusable for reachability-matrix reconstruction problems.

tags.evidence: The hidden graph is an orientation of a tree, and the final reconstruction must be verified as a tree.
The output is an explicit orientation/reconstructed edge list satisfying the matrix.
The easy version directly checks all triples to determine whether an intermediate vertex exists.
